10.1016/j.jmp.2019.102314
In knowledge space theory (KST;  Doignon & Falmagne, 1985, 1999; Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 ) some basic concepts are defined: the knowledge state is the set of all problems that a student masters about a specific knowledge domain; a knowledge structure, is the collection of all possible knowledge states in a population and it always includes at least the empty set and the total set. KST is based on a number of deterministic properties, to which some probabilistic models are applied.

The basic local independence model (BLIM;  Doignon & Falmagne, 1999; Falmagne & Doignon, 1988a ) is one of the most widely applied probabilistic models in KST. It is used for the stochastic assessment of knowledge, and for the empirical validation of knowledge structures. This model has lately received some attention ( de Chiusole, Stefanutti, Anselmi, & Robusto, 2013, 2015; Heller, 2017; Schrepp, 2005; Stefanutti & Robusto, 2009 ), particularly concerning its identifiability.

More specifically,  Spoto, Stefanutti, and Vidotto (2012, 2013)  and  Stefanutti, Spoto, and Vidotto (2018)  have shown that the BLIM is not identifiable for two broad classes of knowledge structures named, respectively, forward-graded and backward-graded. In those articles the analysis was referred to gradedness in either one item or a set of items. It was shown that, under gradedness in an item, there exist more than one set of parameter values of the BLIM predicting the same probability distribution on the observed response patterns and, for this reason, the BLIM is not identifiable.

The type of unidentifiability that occurs with forward- and backward-graded knowledge structures can be described as a property of a commutative group of continuous transformations (named the group of forward and backward transformations) acting on the parameter space of the BLIM. The values of the prediction function of the BLIM are invariant with respect to any of the transformations in the group.

Each of the forward and backward transformations maps the real line to the BLIM’s parameter space and it is bijective in the coordinate of the single item parameter. As a consequence, the number of dimensions of the transformation group (named  trade-off dimensions ) equals the number of unidentifiable item parameters of the BLIM. Since forward and backward transformations are independent of one another, such kind of independence also holds among the unidentifiable item parameters.

The aforementioned theoretical results concerning the identifiability of the BLIM are easily transferred to a rather important model in the cognitive diagnostic models area, namely the Deterministic Input Noisy AND-gate model (DINA,  Haertel, 1984, 1989, 1990 ), as recently pointed out by  Heller, Stefanutti, Anselmi, and Robusto (2015)  and  Stefanutti et al. (2018) . This generalization holds true also with respect to the results presented in this article.

Each element in the group of forward and backward transformations has the property of moving a point in the parameter space of the model, while keeping constant the value of the model’s prediction function (outcome invariance). However, under certain constraints on the model’s parameters, some of the transformations in the group might lose the outcome invariance property. The type of constraints considered in this article are those that fix the probability of a knowledge state to a constant value.

Once the probability of a knowledge state is fixed, only a subset of the whole group of transformations keeps satisfying the outcome invariance property. These are precisely the transformations that are ineffective with respect to the probability of the considered state (i.e., they do not move the point along the coordinate represented by the state probability). In this article we show that, under rather general conditions, nontrivial ineffective transformations can be obtained as compositions of simpler effective ones. A property of such ineffective transformations is that they impose a dependence among the trade-off dimensions and, thus, a dependence among the unidentifiable item parameters.

One way to cope with the unidentifiability of a model is to set (some of) the unidentifiable parameters to constant values. In some cases this strategy is useless, because of the existence of outcome preserving transformations that are ineffective at the parameters at issue. The focus of the present article is on transformations of this type. The study of such type of transformations provides the basis for going beyond forward and backward gradedness, as (un)identifiability conditions of the BLIM.

The article is organized as follows: Section  2  summarizes the fundamental concepts of KST of the BLIM and forward- and backward-graded structures; Section  3  is a brief recap of the existing theoretical results about the identifiability of the BLIM, with a special attention to the very last results concerning transformation groups, gradedness in a set, and tradeoff dimensions; Section  4  introduces the crucial concept of coordinatewise invariance and contains the main theoretical results about the existence of effective and invariant transformations. In Section  6  the new theoretical results are discussed.

A  knowledge domain  is the set  
                         Q 
                       of all problems that can be formulated in a specific topic. A  knowledge state  is the set  
                         
                            K 
                            ⊆ 
                            Q 
                         
                       of all those problems that an individual is able to solve. A  knowledge structure  is a pair

                            , 
                            K

                         K 
                       is a collection of knowledge states, containing at least  
                         0̸

                         Q 
                      . Throughout the article the set  
                         Q 
                       is assumed to be finite.

The four collections defined above play a crucial role in the subsequent parts of this article. It is important to stress that FG and BG knowledge structures are dual to one another. This means that, if  
                         K 
                       is FG in an item  
                         q 
                      , then its dual  
                         
                            
                               
                                  K

                               Q

                               K 
                               :

                               ∈ 
                               K 
                               } 
                            
                         
                       is BG in  
                         q 
                      .

A knowledge state  
                         K 
                       can be regarded as a latent object whose observable counterpart is the  response pattern  
                      
                         
                            R 
                            ⊆ 
                            Q 
                         
                      , representing the set of problems that an individual answered correctly. The observed responses may be noisy due to lucky guesses or careless errors, thus the response pattern  
                         R 
                       of an individual could differ from his/her knowledge state  
                         K 
                      . For this reason, the link between states and patterns has to be modeled by introducing some probabilistic concepts. A  probabilistic knowledge structure  (PKS;  Doignon & Falmagne, 1999 ) is a triple  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            K 
                            , 
                            π 
                            ) 
                         
                       where  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            K 
                            ) 
                         
                       is a knowledge structure and  
                         π 
                       is a probability distribution on  
                         K 
                      . The probability of a response pattern  
                         R 
                       is defined ( Falmagne & Doignon, 1988b ) by the following unrestricted latent class model:  
                         
                            (1) 
                            
                               
                                  P 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     R 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        ∑ 
                                     
                                     
                                        K 
                                        ∈ 
                                        K 
                                     
                                  
                                  P 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     R 
                                     | 
                                     K 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        π 
                                     
                                     
                                        K 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                               
                            
                         
                      where  
                         
                            P 
                            
                               ( 
                               R 
                               | 
                               K 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       is the conditional probability of the response pattern  
                         R 
                       given the knowledge state  
                         K 
                      , and  
                         
                            
                               π 
                            
                            
                               K 
                            
                         
                       is the probability of  
                         K 
                      .

The BLIM is a probabilistic knowledge structure with additional restrictions: the answers to the items are locally independent given the knowledge state of the person; the conditional probability of a pattern given a state is determined by two parameters of each item  
                         q 
                      , a parameter  
                         
                            
                               β 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                         
                       ( careless error ) and a parameter  
                         
                            
                               η 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                         
                       ( lucky guess ) respectively. Under these restrictions the conditional probability  
                         
                            P 
                            
                               ( 
                               R 
                               | 
                               K 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       turns out to be  
                         
                            (2) 
                            
                               
                                  P 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     R 
                                     | 
                                     K 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              ∏ 
                                           
                                           
                                              q 
                                              ∈ 
                                              K 
                                              ∖ 
                                              R 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           
                                              β 
                                           
                                           
                                              q 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              ∏ 
                                           
                                           
                                              q 
                                              ∈ 
                                              K 
                                              ∩ 
                                              R 
                                              
                                                 ∖ 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           1 
                                           − 
                                           
                                              
                                                 β 
                                              
                                              
                                                 q 
                                              
                                           
                                           ) 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              ∏ 
                                           
                                           
                                              q 
                                              ∈ 
                                              R 
                                              ∖ 
                                              K 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           
                                              η 
                                           
                                           
                                              q 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  × 
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              ∏ 
                                           
                                           
                                              q 
                                              ∈ 
                                              Q 
                                              ∖ 
                                              
                                                 ( 
                                                 K 
                                                 ∪ 
                                                 R 
                                                 ) 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           1 
                                           − 
                                           
                                              
                                                 η 
                                              
                                              
                                                 q 
                                              
                                           
                                           ) 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  . 
                               
                            
                         
                       Lucky guess and careless error parameters are expected to be low (see e.g.,  Stefanutti & Robusto, 2009 ). A reasonable assumption is that  
                         
                            
                               
                                  β 
                               
                               
                                  q 
                               
                            
                            + 
                            
                               
                                  η 
                               
                               
                                  q 
                               
                            
                            < 
                            1 
                         
                       for all  
                         
                            q 
                            ∈ 
                            Q 
                         
                       ( Spoto et al., 2012, 2013; Stefanutti, Heller, Anselmi, & Robusto, 2012 ). This assumption stays true throughout the present article.

A probabilistic model with  
                         m 
                       parameters and  
                         n 
                      -dimensional outcomes can be described as a triple  
                         
                            ( 
                            Θ 
                            , 
                            Ω 
                            , 
                            ϕ 
                            ) 
                         
                       where  
                         
                            Θ 
                            ⊆ 
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  m 
                               
                            
                         
                       is the parameter space of the model,  
                         
                            Ω 
                            ⊆ 
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                               
                            
                         
                       is the outcome space, and  
                         
                            ϕ 
                            : 
                            Θ 
                            → 
                            Ω 
                         
                       is the  prediction function  assigning to each parameter vector  
                         
                            θ 
                            ∈ 
                            Θ 
                         
                       a corresponding vector  
                         
                            ω 
                            ∈ 
                            Ω 
                         
                       in the outcome space. A model is said to be (globally) identifiable whenever  
                         ϕ 
                       is injective. It is said to be  locally identifiable  at a point  
                         
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                            ∈ 
                            Θ 
                         
                       if the prediction function  
                         ϕ 
                       is injective when restricted to points  
                         
                            θ 
                            ∈ 
                            Θ 
                         
                       with distance less than some  
                         
                            ϵ 
                            > 
                            0 
                         
                       from  
                         
                            
                               θ 
                            
                            
                               0 
                            
                         
                       ( Bamber & Van Santen, 2000 ). It should be noticed that a globally identifiable model is always locally identifiable at any point of the parameter space, although the contrary need not be true ( Doignon, Heller, & Stefanutti, 2018 ).

In the special case of the BLIM, a point in the parameter space is a vector  
                         θ 
                       containing the  
                         
                            
                               
                                  β 
                               
                               
                                  q 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  η 
                               
                               
                                  q 
                               
                            
                            ∈ 
                            
                               ( 
                               0 
                               , 
                               1 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       values for each item and a probability  
                         
                            
                               
                                  π 
                               
                               
                                  K 
                               
                            
                            ∈ 
                            
                               ( 
                               0 
                               , 
                               1 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       for each knowledge state  
                         
                            K 
                            ∈ 
                            K 
                         
                      , with the two restrictions: (1)  
                         
                            
                               
                                  ∑ 
                               
                               
                                  K 
                                  ∈ 
                                  K 
                               
                            
                            
                               
                                  π 
                               
                               
                                  K 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            1 
                         
                      , and (2)  
                         
                            
                               
                                  β 
                               
                               
                                  q 
                               
                            
                            + 
                            
                               
                                  η 
                               
                               
                                  q 
                               
                            
                            < 
                            1 
                         
                       for all  
                         
                            q 
                            ∈ 
                            Q 
                         
                      .

Let  
                         ϕ 
                       denote the prediction function of the BLIM for a fixed, finite knowledge structure  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            K 
                            ) 
                         
                      . Given a parameter vector  
                         
                            θ 
                            ∈ 
                            Θ 
                         
                      ,  
                         
                            ϕ 
                            
                               ( 
                               θ 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       is a probability distribution on the response patterns. For  
                         
                            R 
                            ⊆ 
                            Q 
                         
                      , the prediction function is  
                         
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        ϕ 
                                     
                                     
                                        R 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     θ 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        ∑ 
                                     
                                     
                                        K 
                                        ∈ 
                                        K 
                                     
                                  
                                  P 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     R 
                                     | 
                                     K 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        π 
                                     
                                     
                                        K 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                               
                            
                         
                      which corresponds to Eq.  (1)  of the BLIM. Identifiability of the BLIM strongly depends on the structure  
                         K 
                       to which it is applied. It is well-known that the BLIM is not globally identifiable in general (see, e.g.,  Heller, 2017 ).  Spoto et al. (2012, 2013)  pointed out that the BLIM is not even locally identifiable for knowledge structures that are either FG or BG in one item. In particular, it was shown that, under gradedness in an item  
                         q 
                      , there exist more than one set of parameter values of the BLIM predicting the same probability distribution on the observed response patterns and, for this reason, the BLIM is not identifiable. These theoretical findings were further investigated and generalized by  Stefanutti et al. (2018)  with the aim of covering the general situation in which a knowledge structure is graded in a set of items. Moreover the authors described the type of unidentifiability of the BLIM that occurs with FG and BG structures as a property of certain kinds of continuous transformations acting on the BLIM’s parameter space. The set of such transformations forms a commutative group under the function composition operator. The values of the prediction function (points in the outcome space) of the BLIM are invariant with respect to anyone transformation in the group. For this reason such transformations are named  outcome preserving . This transformational method can be used to test identifiability at both local and global levels. The backward and forward parameter transformations are defined as follows.

                         For every item  
                               
                                  q 
                                  ∈ 
                                  Q

                                        b

                                  R

Backward transformations change the

A transformation is said to be  effective  whenever it actually moves a point in the parameter space mapping it to another point; whenever this second point is still inside the parameter space, the transformation is said to be  inner . The two pairs  
                         
                            ( 
                            
                               
                                  B 
                               
                               
                                  q 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            ∘ 
                            ) 
                         
                       and  
                         
                            ( 
                            
                               
                                  F 
                               
                               
                                  q 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            ∘ 
                            ) 
                         
                      , where  
                         ∘ 
                       denotes function composition, are  abelian groups  and they can be combined to obtain larger groups.

When some of the parameters of a model are not identifiable, one of the possible attempts to restore identifiability consists in introducing equality constraints in the model. Such constraints, in their simplest form, set the values of the unidentifiable parameters to constant. To give an example, if the knowledge structure is known to be FG in an item  
                         q 
                      , then the  
                         
                            
                               η 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                         
                       parameter might be set equal to some constant value. Obviously, a constraint of this type can resolve the identifiability only if it is applied to an unidentifiable parameter (if one would set  
                         
                            
                               β 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                         
                       to constant, rather then  
                         
                            
                               η 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                         
                      , this last would remain unidentifiable). It is important to observe that the operation consisting of fixing to constant an unidentifiable parameter only works if, after the introduction of the constraint, the parameter at issue becomes invariant with respect to any possible outcome preserving transformation. Stated differently, a parameter is identifiable if and only if it is invariant with respect to any possible outcome preserving transformation.

Given a certain point  
                         
                            θ 
                            ∈ 
                            Θ 
                         
                      , forward and backward parameter transformations and their compositions change the value of some of the parameters (coordinates) in  
                         θ 
                      , while leaving unchanged others. For instance, the forward transformation  
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                            
                               x 
                            
                         
                      , with  
                         
                            q 
                            ∈ 
                            Q 
                         
                       and  
                         
                            x 
                            ∈ 
                            R 
                         
                      , does not change the value of  
                         
                            
                               β 
                            
                            
                               r 
                            
                         
                       for all  
                         
                            r 
                            ∈ 
                            Q 
                         
                      . Moreover, if  
                         
                            K 
                            ∈ 
                            K 
                            ∖ 
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           K 
                                        
                                        
                                           ̄ 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     q 
                                  
                               
                               ∪ 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           K 
                                        
                                        
                                           ̄ 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     q 
                                  
                                  
                                     + 
                                  
                               
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       then  
                         
                            
                               π 
                            
                            
                               K 
                            
                         
                       is not changed by the transformation. We say, in this case, that the coordinate  
                         
                            
                               π 
                            
                            
                               K 
                            
                         
                       of  
                         θ 
                       is  invariant  with respect to the transformation  
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                            
                               x 
                            
                         
                       or, in general, that  
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                            
                               x

                               π 
                            
                            
                               K 
                            
                         
                      . It is important to keep in mind that the unidentifiable parameters of the BLIM are not invariant with respect to some outcome preserving transformation.

If a transformation  
                         
                            t 
                            = 
                            
                               
                                  t

                      . However, as it is shown in this section, the opposite implication does not hold in general. Namely, a parameter

                       in the composition.

                          and its dual, leading to rows 2, 5, 6, and 7 of  Table 1 .

There are in the whole seven lemmas, covering all cases represented by rows in  Table 1  and showing invariance of a certain  
                            
                               
                                  π 
                               
                               
                                  K 
                               
                            
                          with respect to the transformation compositions. For instance the first lemma is about the case of a composition of two forward transformations (and its dual case) with  
                            K 
                          belonging to both  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        K 
                                     
                                     
                                        ̄ 
                                     
                                  
                               
                               
                                  q 
                               
                               
                                  + 
                               
                            
                          and  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        K 
                                     
                                     
                                        ̄ 
                                     
                                  
                               
                               
                                  r 
                               
                               
                                  − 
                               
                            
                         , for  
                            
                               q 
                               , 
                               r 
                               ∈ 
                               F 
                            
                         . A final theorem provides the main result of this section.

We observe that  Lemmas 1 and 3  exclude that  
                            q 
                          and  
                            r 
                          are the same item. This is because the resulting composition has form  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     f 
                                  
                                  
                                     q 
                                  
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                               
                               ∘ 
                               
                                  
                                     f 
                                  
                                  
                                     q 
                                  
                                  
                                     y 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     f 
                                  
                                  
                                     q 
                                  
                                  
                                     x 
                                     + 
                                     y 
                                  
                               
                               ∈ 
                               
                                  
                                     F 
                                  
                                  
                                     q 
                                  
                               
                            
                          and hence it is just one of the elementary forward transformations ( Stefanutti et al., 2018 ). Since  
                            
                               
                                  π 
                               
                               
                                  K 
                               
                            
                          is not invariant with respect to any of the transformations in the group, this special case is of no interest here (see also  Remark 1 ). In  Lemmas 2, 4 and 5  the condition  
                            
                               q 
                               ≠ 
                               r 
                            
                          implicitly holds true, for otherwise the collection of states to which the transformation can be applied would be empty. Finally,  Lemmas 6 and 7  hold in both cases  
                            
                               q 
                               = 
                               r 
                            
                          and  
                            
                               q 
                               ≠ 
                               r 
                            
                         .

Since the proofs of all the seven Lemmas follow very similar lines of reasoning, in the text we present only the proof for  Lemma 1 , while all the other proofs can be found in  Appendix .

                                     0̸ 
                                  
                               , and there is no state  
                                  K 
                                satisfying the lemma’s conditions.

The next theorem makes use of the seven lemmas above and provides a general result, valid across cases, in which it is established that the types of transformations that keep constant a state probability are continuous and inner.

Concerning (ii) and (iii), the cases described in the seven Lemmas are considered separately. In the two cases described by  Lemma 1 , in view of establishing that

                                  g 
                                is real and continuous. For both cases in  Lemma 1 , two distinct asymptotes are obtained by setting to zero the numerator and the denominator of the argument of the logarithm in Eq.  (3) . The theoretical results provided in the previous sections may be useful in practice for determining subsets of parameters that could be set fixed for resolving the unidentifiability of the BLIM. In this section an example is illustrated that could help clarifying this aspect.

In some cases there could be reasons for fixing some of the knowledge state probabilities  
                         
                            
                               π 
                            
                            
                               K 
                            
                         
                       to a constant value. For instance a knowledge state could be so rare in the population that its probability is close to zero. It could be reasonable to fix the value of this state to a very small probability.

                         K

                      .

It is known that the BLIM is not identifiable in general ( Spoto et al., 2012, 2013 ). Its identifiability strictly depends on the properties of the knowledge structure to which it is applied. If the knowledge structure is either forward- or backward-graded in one or more items, then the BLIM is not identifiable. In such cases, there exist continuous transformations of the model’s parameters, named forward and backward transformations, that keep constant the value of the model’s prediction function. The collection of all such transformations forms a commutative group that acts on the BLIM’s parameter space ( Stefanutti et al., 2018 ) .

A possible way for resolving the unidentifiability of the model consists of fixing one or more of its parameters to a constant value, thus reducing by one or more dimensions the parameter space. The choice of such parameters cannot be arbitrary, however. It has to be considered that fixing any one of the parameters to constant can reduce by at most one the number of trade-off dimensions. Therefore, some of the parameters might not reduce the number of trade-off dimensions. The trade-off graph of the knowledge structure allows to establish which of the  
                         
                            
                               π 
                            
                            
                               K 
                            
                         
                       parameters can reduce the number of trade-off dimensions, when fixed to constant. It was shown that each of the states in the knowledge structure belongs to one of the following three classes:

(A) 
                            states whose probability  
                                  
                                     
                                        π 
                                     
                                     
                                        K 
                                     
                                  
                                is invariant with respect to the whole group of transformations; 
                         
                         
                            (B) 
                            states for which the relevant transformation is either forward or backward in a single item; 
                         
                         
                            (C) 
                            states that are involved in more than a single trade-off dimension.

states whose probability  
                                  
                                     
                                        π 
                                     
                                     
                                        K 
                                     
                                  
                                is invariant with respect to the whole group of transformations;

states for which the relevant transformation is either forward or backward in a single item;

states that are involved in more than a single trade-off dimension.

Concerning class (B), if the probability  
                         
                            
                               π 
                            
                            
                               K 
                            
                         
                       of such states is fixed to constant, then the transformation can no longer be applied without modifying the response pattern distribution. As a consequence the number of trade-off dimensions decreases by one and the corresponding item parameter becomes identifiable. On the other hand, with respect to the states in class (C), if the probability  
                         
                            
                               π 
                            
                            
                               K 
                            
                         
                       of one of them is fixed to a constant value, the number of trade-off dimensions decreases by one, but none of the corresponding item parameters becomes identifiable.

Interestingly enough, the identifiability issues observed for the BLIM can be observed in some of the models belonging to the framework of the cognitive diagnostic modeling. In fact, while  Heller et al. (2015)  established a precise correspondence between the BLIM and the DINA, DINO and MS-DINA models,  Stefanutti et al. (2018)  suggested that some of the identifiability issues described for the BLIM are immediately extended to the probabilistic models applied in CDMs. In recent years a number of research papers explored identifiability in CDMs (e.g.  Chen, Liu, Xu, & Ying, 2015; Gu & Xu, 2019; Liu, Xu, & Ying, 2013; Xu, 2017; Xu & Shang, 2018 ). Nonetheless, some remarks in this respect must be raised. In the first place, the notion of a knowledge structure turns out to be more general than that of a  Q -matrix. In fact, it was shown by  Heller et al. (2015)  that, in the conjunctive case, the knowledge structures delineated by a  Q -matrix are all closed under intersection (in the disjunctive case, they are closed under union). As a consequence, the identifiability conditions established for a  Q -matrix are only valid for a special class of knowledge structures, not for all. In the second place, the conditions established by  Gu and Xu (2019)  are valid as long as  global identifiability  is concerned. However, this last is much stronger than local identifiability. In fact, even if a model is not globally identifiable, it could still be locally identifiable, and this is a sufficient requirement for having consistent model parameter estimates. Finally, necessary and sufficient conditions for local identifiability are missing in both CDMs and KST. This article contributes in this direction.

To conclude, the theoretical results provided in this article shed further light to the issue of unidentifiability of the BLIM. However, from a practical point of view, it could be difficult to justify the choice of an arbitrary probability value for any of the  
                         
                            
                               π 
                            
                            
                               K 
                            
                         
                       parameters, to the only sake of having an identifiable model. A special case is represented by a probability value of  
                         
                            
                               π 
                            
                            
                               K 
                            
                         
                       fixed to zero. Setting the probability of a knowledge state to zero essentially amounts to removing the state from the structure, which appears to be a more plausible choice. This choice however is not covered by the theoretical results provided in this article, since the parameter space is assumed to be an open set (which excludes zeros). Nonetheless this could be the subject matter of future studies.

This work was carried out within the scope of the project “use-inspired basic research”, for which the Department of General Psychology of the University of Padova has been recognized as “Dipartimento di Eccellenza” by the Italian Ministry of University and Research. Furthermore, the research carried out in this article was supported by a grant to Luca Stefanutti in the context of the project  BIRD181573/18  of the  University of Padova, Italy 
 .

In this appendix the proofs of Lemmas from 2 to 7 are provided.
